cues.title: Watch the motion
cues.text: Watch these two skaters. Their rolling platforms are still. They press their palms together, and both begin to glide apart. Here is the mystery: where did the force come from? Do not answer with a single object. Look for a pair.

cues.title: A force needs two
cues.text: A force is a push or pull in an interaction between two objects. The first skater pushes on the second skater. At the same time, the second skater pushes on the first. One interaction, two objects, and a force on each object.

cues.title: Name both objects
cues.text: Our arrows must tell the whole story. This gold arrow means skater A on skater B. The matching arrow means skater B on skater A. The arrows point in opposite directions because each skater is pushed away from the contact.

cues.title: Motion is evidence
cues.text: Now compare before and after. Before contact, both platforms were still. After the push, the skaters move apart. That change in motion is evidence that forces acted. We did not see a force by itself; we saw what the forces changed.

cues.title: The push has ended
cues.text: Here is a common trap: saying the push is stored inside a skater and rides along. It does not. Once their hands separate, that hand-to-hand force has ended. The skaters keep moving for a while, but the contact push is no longer acting.

cues.title: Push or pull
cues.text: Contact forces can push or pull. A hand pushes a cart. A rope pulls a trolley. In each case, name both objects: hand on cart, or rope on trolley. If you can name only one object, your explanation is not finished.

cues.title: No touch required
cues.text: Some forces act without the objects touching. These magnet carts have a clear air gap, yet their motion can change. The interaction is magnet on magnet. Gravity is another non-contact interaction: Earth pulls on a book even when the book is falling through the air.

cues.title: Contact or non-contact
cues.text: Use two questions. Which two objects interact? Do they touch? Foot on ball is contact. Magnet on magnet can be non-contact. Earth on ball is non-contact. The label comes from the evidence, not from whether the force looks strong or weak.

cues.title: Test one change
cues.text: Suppose we want to compare two pushes. Use the same cart and the same surface, then change only the push. Measure how far the cart travels. If we change the cart, surface and push together, the result cannot tell us which change mattered.

cues.title: Repair the explanation
cues.text: Try this explanation: the ball moved because it had force inside it. Repair it. A foot pushed on the ball during contact, so the ball's motion changed. After the foot left, that contact force ended. Exact object pairs make the explanation stronger.

cues.title: Predict the evidence
cues.text: Final challenge. Two magnet carts begin still with a gap between them. They move apart. Which evidence supports a non-contact push? The gap stayed visible, both motions changed, and no hand touched either cart. That is a claim supported by observations.

cues.title: Your physics move
cues.text: Your Physics move is simple and powerful: name the two objects, decide whether they touch, then use the change in motion as evidence. Force is not a substance hiding inside an object. Force is an interaction. You are ready for the cockpit check.

actions.narration: Watch these two skaters. Their rolling platforms are still. They press their palms together, and both begin to glide apart. Here is the mystery: where did the force come from? Do not answer with a single object. Look for a pair.

A force is a push or pull in an interaction between two objects. The first skater pushes on the second skater. At the same time, the second skater pushes on the first. One interaction, two objects, and a force on each object.

Our arrows must tell the whole story. This gold arrow means skater A on skater B. The matching arrow means skater B on skater A. The arrows point in opposite directions because each skater is pushed away from the contact.

Now compare before and after. Before contact, both platforms were still. After the push, the skaters move apart. That change in motion is evidence that forces acted. We did not see a force by itself; we saw what the forces changed.

Here is a common trap: saying the push is stored inside a skater and rides along. It does not. Once their hands separate, that hand-to-hand force has ended. The skaters keep moving for a while, but the contact push is no longer acting.

Contact forces can push or pull. A hand pushes a cart. A rope pulls a trolley. In each case, name both objects: hand on cart, or rope on trolley. If you can name only one object, your explanation is not finished.

Some forces act without the objects touching. These magnet carts have a clear air gap, yet their motion can change. The interaction is magnet on magnet. Gravity is another non-contact interaction: Earth pulls on a book even when the book is falling through the air.

Use two questions. Which two objects interact? Do they touch? Foot on ball is contact. Magnet on magnet can be non-contact. Earth on ball is non-contact. The label comes from the evidence, not from whether the force looks strong or weak.

Suppose we want to compare two pushes. Use the same cart and the same surface, then change only the push. Measure how far the cart travels. If we change the cart, surface and push together, the result cannot tell us which change mattered.

Try this explanation: the ball moved because it had force inside it. Repair it. A foot pushed on the ball during contact, so the ball's motion changed. After the foot left, that contact force ended. Exact object pairs make the explanation stronger.

Final challenge. Two magnet carts begin still with a gap between them. They move apart. Which evidence supports a non-contact push? The gap stayed visible, both motions changed, and no hand touched either cart. That is a claim supported by observations.

Your Physics move is simple and powerful: name the two objects, decide whether they touch, then use the change in motion as evidence. Force is not a substance hiding inside an object. Force is an interaction. You are ready for the cockpit check.
